### Post by knichel on 2007-09-10
I am fairly new to iptables.  I have a server with 2 NIC's Eth0 is internal and Eth1 is external.  have successfully hijacked the internal outbound traffic and forwarded it to dans guardian (content filer) then that goes to squid (proxy).  The problem I am having is that from outside, I cannot http to the webserver.  Here is my iptables config...

======================================================
# Generated by iptables-save v1.3.6 on Mon Sep 10 13:11:20 2007
*nat
:PREROUTING ACCEPT [81:7878]
:POSTROUTING ACCEPT [121:7260]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [226:13560]
-A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 80 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.6.200:8080 
-A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 3128 -j REDIRECT --to-ports 8080 
-A POSTROUTING -o eth1 -j MASQUERADE 
COMMIT
# Completed on Mon Sep 10 13:11:20 2007
# Generated by iptables-save v1.3.6 on Mon Sep 10 13:11:20 2007
*mangle
:PREROUTING ACCEPT [4366:1994290]
:INPUT ACCEPT [4345:1992793]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [21:1497]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [4112:2590378]
:POSTROUTING ACCEPT [4133:2591875]
COMMIT
# Completed on Mon Sep 10 13:11:20 2007
# Generated by iptables-save v1.3.6 on Mon Sep 10 13:11:20 2007
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD ACCEPT [7:671]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [1131:285897]
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -i eth1 -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -i eth0 -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -j LOG 
-A INPUT -j DROP 
-A FORWARD -i eth0 -j ACCEPT 
-A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT 
-A OUTPUT -o eth0 -j ACCEPT 
COMMIT
# Completed on Mon Sep 10 13:11:20 2007
======================================================

Any ideas?

---

### Post by kidders on 2007-09-11
Hi there,

I may be misreading your rules, but ...

> **knichel said:**
> :INPUT DROP [0:0]
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -i eth1 -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -i eth0 -j ACCEPT 
-A INPUT -j LOG 
-A INPUT -j DROPThere doesn't seem to be a rule that would admit packets on eth1 to a local web server. You do have one that would allow packets related to pre-existing connections, but none that lets *unsolicited* data in on port 80 (ie to form a connection to your web server in the first place).

---
